Title: Gulf conflict sends oil prices higher, ripple effects felt across global economy
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The escalation of conflict in the Gulf and disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz have driven sharp increases in global oil prices, with impacts extending far beyond the gas pump.
According to expert insights and scholars from the Kleinman Center for Energy Policy and Perry World House, the latest energy price shocks are reshaping economic stability, global development, geopolitics and the pace of the clean energy transition.
Rising energy costs are already affecting transportation, food prices, electricity bills, infrastructure spending, and household affordability worldwide, adding pressure across both developed and emerging economies.
Sanya Carley, faculty director of the Kleinman Centers, said the impact is seen as immediate and tangible at the household level.
"High oil prices mean that people pay more at the pump," she said.
She said that at an average gasoline price of about US$2.94 per gallon (RM11.66 per 3.785 litres) in January, filling a 15-gallon tank (56.78 litres) would have cost around US$44 (RM174.57), excluding tax.
By early May, with the average price rising to US$4.52 per gallon (RM17.93), the same tank would cost about US$68 (RM269.80) to fill, an increase of US$24 (RM95.22), also before taxes.
Carley said that for households dependent on personal vehicles, particularly those commuting longer distances due to housing affordability constraints, these additional costs can quickly accumulate and force difficult trade-offs.
She warned that households under financial strain may respond by taking on debt or cutting back on essential spending such as food, utilities and healthcare.
Carley also said higher oil prices ripple through the broader economy as transportation and logistics costs rise, with businesses passing at least part of those increases on to consumers while smaller firms face additional pressure on already thin margins.
The strain is compounded by broader concerns about household energy insecurity.
Energy justice researcher Alison Knasin highlighted that higher fuel costs reduce disposable income available for essentials such as food, medicine, rent and utilities, increasing the risk of utility bill arrears and service disconnections.
She noted that such disruptions are strongly associated with poorer physical and mental health outcomes and said that rising oil prices could deepen energy insecurity, particularly when households are simultaneously facing pressures across housing, food and other basic needs.
Beyond households, the macroeconomic effects are increasingly evident.
Climate and development expert Stephen Hammer pointed to the cumulative impact of recent global shocks, including the energy-driven inflation following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and the fiscal strain from the Covid-19 period, which have already reduced government fiscal space and increased debt servicing costs.
He said the latest energy price shock linked to geopolitical tensions has further compounded these pressures, pushing up costs for gasoline, diesel, food and consumer goods while weakening public finances.
In several cases, this has translated into delayed or deferred infrastructure investment, as rising project costs and tighter financing conditions crowd out long-term development priorities.
Hammer also noted that reduced access to concessional development finance and higher borrowing costs have made it more expensive for governments and municipalities to fund infrastructure, with many local authorities now citing rising project costs as a primary constraint on delivery.
This is affecting investments in areas such as water systems, public transit and climate resilience, which are increasingly postponed rather than cancelled due to financing constraints.
The transmission mechanism of electricity prices further amplifies the impact of energy market volatility.
Energy law expert James Van Nostrand explained that in the United States, natural gas accounts for about 40 per cent of electricity generation, meaning higher gas prices directly raise the cost of power production.
He added that the indirect effect is even more significant in competitive wholesale electricity markets, where natural gas-fired plants often set the marginal price of electricity.
In many regions, gas serves as the "price setter" for electricity more than 90 per cent of the time, meaning increases in natural gas prices can raise wholesale electricity prices across the system, ultimately feeding through to retail consumers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Job ad for shepherds goes viral in China exposing labour market strains
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Chinese farm owner Zuo Xiaoyong was stunned to see his job ad for shepherds to work in the remote and rugged grasslands south of Mongolia becoming the day's top trending social media post.
More than 700 people applied for the two positions, including white-collar employees from megacities Shanghai and Chongqing, factory workers across China, and even university graduates.
The response to Zuo's late April ad - which drew 59 million views within hours on Weibo, China's version of X, where it generated 21,000 different discussion threads - reveals the growing strains in the country's job market.
"I didn't expect it to go viral," said Zuo, adding that a 10th of the applicants had just finished university, while others had debt, grinding industrial jobs, or were worn down by workplace politics.
"It seems ordinary people are having a hard time finding work."
CUT-THROAT, LOW-REWARD JOB MARKET
While headline unemployment has hovered just above 5.0 per cent, underemployment in China is rising, and private sector incomes have lagged economic growth for most of the past decade.
Blue- and white-collar workers alike complain about the '996' culture of working 9am to 9pm, six days a week.
Analysts expect the labour market to worsen in coming months, as factories face higher costs due to the Iran war, while AI adoption accelerates, and a record 12.7 million university graduates this summer begin job hunting.
The reaction to Zuo's ad was "symptomatic of what continues to be a highly competitive and often low-rewarding labour market," said Lynn Song, chief China economist at ING.
"Urban jobs are becoming less attractive and more rare." China's 5.0 per cent economic growth relies heavily on surging exports, as manufacturers sacrifice profits to gain market share worldwide, putting more pressure on workers back home.
James Guo applied for the job because he was exhausted by his work at a factory making shipping containers.
"You have no idea what it's like to work more than 13 hours a day, fastening screws until your hands are swollen and covered in blisters, without even having time to go to the bathroom," the 21-year-old said.
"The workload is too intense, I can't take it anymore." Zuo was looking for shepherds, preferably a couple, to take 3,000 sheep out to graze on a 2,000-hectare (4,942 acres) pasture in the summer, and undertake strenuous indoor feeding and cleaning during the winter when temperatures can drop below minus 30 C (minus 22 F).
For that, the shepherds would each get 8,000 yuan (US$1,178) per month, well above the national urban average in private companies of roughly 6,000 yuan, and have accommodation and groceries provided.
Shaun Rein, managing director of China Market Research Group, said master's degree holders from top universities sought similar salaries in Shanghai, but most of the income would vanish on renting a tiny apartment and paying for other basic expenses.
Zuo, who also owns 200 cattle, said the pay matched the hardship.
"The salary is high, but whether you can work long-term and get through the winter is what matters most," Zuo said. "This is not tourism."
'CURSE OF 35'
Half of the applicants were born in the 1990s, Zuo said, an age group at the centre of what Chinese workers call the 'curse of 35,' with studies showing most employers, including the public sector, overlook candidates older than that.
"We are seeing the 'curse of 35' move from a tech-sector meme to a broader economic reality," said Christian Yao, senior lecturer in human resource management at Victoria University of Wellington.
One white-collar worker in e-commerce, 28-year-old Wu, who gave only her surname for privacy reasons, earns 10,000 yuan a month but the shepherding job piqued her interest.
"I want to escape city life and stop dealing with all kinds of difficult people," Wu said.
"I could enjoy a peaceful, secluded life away from the world." In the end, Zuo hired four shepherds - two couples - who were all born in the 1980s and had previously worked on a farm. While he has kept 40 more couples on a shortlist, he says he will not consider singles or young urbanites for the roles.
"In our place, you might not see people for a whole year," Zuo said. "Whether someone can endure such loneliness, I don't know."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hong Kong overtakes Switzerland as world's top cross-border wealth hub on China ties
Article:
ZURICH: Hong Kong has overtaken Switzerland as the top global booking centre for cross-border wealth, a first that is unlikely to be reversed as hubs in Asia grow faster than the European safe-haven, Boston Consulting Group said on Wednesday.
Wealth from China and an IPO boom in 2025 helped Hong Kong rise to a US$2.95 trillion offshore behemoth for the world's rich, narrowly surpassing Switzerland's US$2.94 trillion in cross-border wealth, according to BCG's 2026 Global Wealth Report.
"Hong Kong is cementing its role as China's gateway to global markets, though that same concentration ties its trajectory tightly to economic and regulatory developments on the mainland," the authors said.
Both Hong Kong and Singapore are projected to continue growing as cross-border booking centres at around 9.0 per cent annually through 2030, compared to an expected 6.0 per cent average in Switzerland over the same period.
Cross-border wealth globally grew 8.4 per cent to US$15.7 trillion last year, driven by strong markets and more demand for geographical diversification, and it flowed overwhelmingly to the world's top 10 booking centres, further boosting concentration, BCG added.
CLIENT PROXIMITY MATTERS
Despite slower growth rates, Switzerland's diversification may prove an advantage as it draws clients from all regions, while the Asian hubs largely depend on growth in China, the report added.
"Geopolitical uncertainty reaffirms Switzerland's role as a core global booking centre, attracting flight-to-safety flows from more volatile regions such as the Middle East," BCG said. Wealthy individuals have been looking to shift assets from the Gulf region to Switzerland in the wake of the ongoing conflict, bankers and financial advisers have told Reuters.
"What ultimately matters is client proximity," said Michael Kahlich, who co-authored the BCG report, adding that two hubs are forming globally - Singapore and Hong Kong for Asia, and Switzerland, the UK, and the US for the Western region.
As being close to clients has become more important, Swiss banks have expanded to other major hubs, Kahlich added. "UBS is number one in wealth management in both Singapore and Hong Kong," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Goldman Sachs lifts S&P 500 year-end target to 8,000 on strong earnings outlook
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Goldman Sachs has raised its 2026 year-end forecast for the S&P 500 index to 8,000 from 7,600, citing continued strength in corporate earnings.
The target is 6.4 per cent higher than the index's last close of 7,519.12.
"Earnings growth has powered the entire S&P 500 return so far this year, and we expect this dynamic to continue in the coming months," Goldman Sachs said in a note on Tuesday.
The brokerage also raised its S&P 500 earnings-per-share forecasts to US$340 for 2026, implying 24 per cent year-on-year growth, and to US$385 for 2027, a further 13 per cent increase. Goldman's move adds to a growing wave of bullish calls from brokerages, with UBS GWM the latest to lift its outlook last week, citing robust AI-driven earnings that could help offset inflationary pressures and supply risks from the Iran conflict.
The brokerage said AI infrastructure beneficiaries are set to drive about half of the index's earnings growth this year, adding that while weak consumer spending and elevated costs pose risks, strong AI investments would offset these pressures.
"In addition, while S&P 500 earnings estimates have risen more quickly than index price appreciation, the semiconductor stocks at the heart of the AI infrastructure complex have recently outpaced their forward earnings," analysts at Goldman Sachs said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 11-month-old baby girl found drowned in pail of soapy water
Article:
SEREMBAN: An 11-month-old baby girl died after she was believed to have drowned in a pail filled with soapy water at a house along Jalan Sri Permata Ladang here yesterday.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Yatim Osman said the infant was found head-down in the pail.
"At 7.22pm yesterday, the Seremban district police headquarters received a report regarding the death of an 11-month-old baby girl at a house.
"Preliminary investigations revealed that the victim was being cared for by her grandmother from 9am on the day of the incident while her parents were away at work," he said in a statement today.
Yatim said the victim had been left asleep in a cradle at 2.30pm while her grandmother was cooking and hanging laundry.
"A short while later, the grandmother realised the victim was no longer in the cradle. After searching the area, she found the child inside a pail containing soapy water at the back of the house, with her head submerged," he said.
The victim was rushed to a nearby clinic before being referred to Tuanku Jaafar Hospital, where she was pronounced dead at 4.30pm.
"A post-mortem examination was conducted at 1am today, but the exact cause of death is pending laboratory investigations," he said.
Following the incident, police detained a 54-year-old woman to assist in investigations under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001.
Those with information regarding the incident can contact investigating officer Inspector R. Sinniah at 06-6033222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ferrari unveils 'Luce', its first fully electric car, in a tough market
Article:
ROME: Italian luxury carmaker Ferrari has unveiled its first fully electric model, the Luce, belatedly joining rivals like Porsche and Lamborghini to offer clean-energy driving thrills.
But the Luce – Italian for "Light" – has yet to win over investors, with Ferrari shares the worst performers on the Milan stock exchange Tuesday, tumbling six percent following a presentation of the new vehicle in Rome.
Renowned for its powerful engines, the prestigious sports car manufacturer already sells nearly half of its cars in hybrid versions. Yet the brand with the prancing horse logo took several years before deciding to throw its hat into the all-electric ring.
The five-seat Luce – which analysts believe could be priced upwards of 700,000 euros ($815,000) – boasts a top speed of over 310 kilometres per hour (190 miles per hour), accelerating to 100 kph in 2.5 seconds, according to Ferrari.
With a massive 122 kWh battery, it offers a range of over 530 kilometres and weighs 2.26 tonnes. With fast charging, the vehicle can reach an 80 percent charge from 10 percent in 20 to 25 minutes, the company claims.
The car is only the second Ferrari model with four doors in the company's history, after the Purosangue SUV, and the first five-seater for a brand better known for its sleek two-seaters.
"This new model carries into the future the values that make Ferrari instantly recognisable all over the world," Ferrari chairman John Elkann said after presenting the car to Italian President Sergio Mattarella in Rome.
He also presented it to Pope Leo XIV, who got to sit in the driver's seat of the parked car at his residence in Castel Gandolfo.
Elkann then "donated the car's steering wheel to the pontiff," Ferrari said.
While the brand developed many of the components in-house, the Luce's design was entrusted to the LoveFrom collective of Jony Ive, who designed many of Apple's most iconic products, and industrial designer Marc Newson.
Ferrari describes its new electric vehicle as a "glass house", with headlights that are invisible when switched off, and a rear end reminiscent of famous models such as the 360 Modena.
Built in a brand-new factory in Maranello, northern Italy, the first cars are scheduled for delivery toward the end of this year.
- Not so fast -
Investors are not yet convinced by Ferrari's shift to electric, which comes as other carmakers have hit the brakes on expanding their electric ranges due to slower takeup than they hoped for.
"We maintain the view that an electric model with a high price tag... will not generate significant volumes" capable of bolstering Ferrari's earnings, Equita analysts wrote in a research note Tuesday.
Banca Akros analysts were more positive, noting that even with the risk of margin dilution, "we believe that the extremely high price (over 700,000 euros after customisation) more than offsets this dilution, with the Luce already expected to be profitable."
Akros noted that Ferrari's chief executive Benedetto Vigna has said he expects half of the Luce orders to come from new clients.
Ferrari's share price had already plummeted in October 2025 when the first details of the Luce were unveiled, with analysts disappointed by a long-term profit outlook that fell short of expectations.
Ferrari scaled back its electric ambitions at the end of 2025, projecting that electric models would account for 20 percent of its lineup in 2030, down from its previous target of 40 percent.
Shares of Ferrari were down 6.3 percent at midday on the Milan stock exchange to 290.45 euros.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar donates sacrificial cow to Negri Sembilan media club for Aidiladha
Article:
SEREMBAN: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has donated a sacrificial cow to media practitioners in Negri Sembilan to perform the ritual in conjunction with Hari Raya Aidiladha.
The contribution gave media practitioners in the state an opportunity to participate in a sacrificial programme for the first time.
The event was organised by the Negri Sembilan Media Club (Manis) in collaboration with the Taman Bandar Senawang Kariah Mosque.
Manis chairman Muhd Danial Amsyar Arfi expressed his appreciation to the prime minister, saying the contribution reflected deep concern for media practitioners in the state.
"This programme also serves as a platform to bring media practitioners closer to the community through social and religious activities. Part of the meat will be distributed to those in need, while the remainder will be given to Manis members.
"Manis hopes programmes such as this can continue in the future as part of social responsibility efforts to assist the underprivileged, while strengthening ties between the media and the community," he said in a statement today.
Taman Bandar Senawang Kariah Mosque chairman Hosnan Selamat said 15 cows, including the contribution from Manis, were sacrificed at the mosque compound in conjunction with the festival.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fahmi thanks Tunku Ismail for support of armed forces
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Communications Minister and government spokesman Datuk Fahmi Fadzil has expressed appreciation to the Regent of Johor, Tunku Ismail Sultan Ibrahim, for reaffirming his unwavering support for the armed forces.
Fahmi said matters relating to military affairs, training exercises, and constitutional issues had already been clarified by the National Security Council (NSC).
He added that the public was well aware of the council's official statement on the matter.
"I humbly express my gratitude for the decree of the Regent of Johor," Fahmi said.
In a Facebook post yesterday, Tunku Ismail said it was deeply regrettable when individuals attempted to use the
military institution
for personal interests or to gain social media attention and public recognition.
The Regent noted that before issuing statements on such matters, one must understand the Federal Constitution, including the historical and legal distinctions between Federated and Non-Federated States.
"A superficial understanding of military service and constitutional matters only reflects a failure to truly comprehend the sacrifices, discipline, responsibilities, and realities endured by members of the armed forces," Tunku Ismail said.
Tunku Ismail said Johor's doors will always remain open to the armed forces, stressing that sacrifices, dedication and service of its personnel in defending the nation must always be respected and appreciated.
He said military training and operational exercises, which are vital to national defence and preparedness, should not be subjected to unnecessary obstacles.
Meanwhile, the armed forces today expressed their gratitude to Tunku Ismail for his unwavering support.
The NSC stated that all military exercises conducted by the armed forces are subject to strict approval and verification procedures involving state authorities and technical agencies.
It said the applications for military exercises – whether in gazetted or non-gazetted training areas – must undergo detailed approval processes before being carried out.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATEDS] Azam to meet police next week over Albert Tei's allegations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki is expected to meet Bukit Aman police next week to assist in investigations into allegations made by businessman Albert Tei.
Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department director Datuk M. Kumar said police had established contact with the individual involved and arrangements were being made to record further statements.
"So far, we have established contact and a meeting will be arranged next week," he said when contacted today.
Azam had previously said he welcomed investigations into the matter and would give his full cooperation to the authorities.
He also said he was
considering legal action
against Tei over what he described as baseless allegations.
Yesterday, Kumar confirmed that Azam would be called up to assist investigations following a police report lodged by Tei on May 13.
The case is being investigated under Section 506 of the Penal Code for criminal intimidation.
Previously, Tei alleged that Azam had threatened, obstructed and victimised him when he attempted to expose an alleged corruption scandal involving several high-profile Sabah politicians last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man found drowned near university not a student or staff
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A man who was found drowned in a lake near a university was neither a student nor a staff member, the university has clarified.
Taylor's University confirmed that the man involved in the drowning incident at a lake within the vicinity of its Lakeside Campus was not affiliated with the university as either a student or staff member.
In a statement posted on its social media page, the university said it was deeply saddened by the incident and noted that the relevant authorities had responded promptly and were currently conducting investigations.
The university added that it is fully cooperating with the authorities.
"Out of respect for those involved and their families, we kindly urge the public to refrain from circulating unverified information, photos or videos related to the incident," the statement said.
Earlier, it was reported that a man was found
drowned
after allegedly falling into the lake on Wednesday.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said the department received an emergency call at 4.48pm, before a team from the Subang Jaya fire station was dispatched to the scene.
Witnesses reportedly saw the man fall into the lake, and firefighters later recovered his body using a grappling hook.
The victim's identity has yet to be established, and the remains were handed over to police for further action.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Frugal and more online: smarter spenders rewrite luxury's China dream
Article:
BEIJING: When Beijinger Jacqueline Li first heard that one of her favourite luxury department stores in the city was closing, she was shocked — until she reflected on how Chinese spending habits have changed in recent years.
The closure of France's Galeries Lafayette in the Chinese capital on Wednesday comes against the backdrop of a sluggish post-pandemic economy and shifting consumer behaviour.
It is the latest sign that foreign luxury brands may no longer command the same appeal — or spending power — they enjoyed in the world's second-largest economy during the 2010s.
"I think people's spending habits after the pandemic may be more cost-effective and practical," Li told AFP during a final visit to the Galeries on its penultimate day of operations.
"It's no longer as over-the-top as before, like needing to have an impressive logo. So you'll see that demand for luxury goods has fallen," said the international school admissions officer.
While luxury spending in Europe and the United States has been supported by savings accumulated during the pandemic, Chinese consumers have become more cautious as the post-Covid recovery has faltered.
The property market, where millions had invested their savings, continues to struggle through a prolonged downturn, while middle-class incomes have stagnated and youth unemployment remains high.
Last month, consumer spending grew at the slowest pace in more than three years, according to official data.
In 2025, China's luxury market declined by three to five per cent after plunging 17 to 19 per cent the previous year, according to consultancy Bain & Company.
PANDEMIC HANGOVER
Before the pandemic, China's expanding middle class created a wave of first-time luxury buyers, said Lisa Nan, editor at Jing Daily, a publication focused on China's luxury market.
Now, "because of the economic downturn, people are much more rational and they have to navigate through this difficult period", she said.
Even in Shanghai's upscale shopping districts, consumers still vividly feel the uncertainty created by the Covid pandemic.
"I find myself wanting to save money even more now," said 24-year-old July Xu, who was shopping with friends in the fashionable Xintiandi district.
"Having lived through such an extraordinary period during the pandemic, I've come to realise that having some personal savings is quite important."
"Beforehand, people felt like money came pretty easily, with their income increasing every year, but during the pandemic a lot of people suddenly lost their jobs," said 61-year-old Hu Shuqing outside a luxury fragrance store.
Some analysts believe there could still be room for a recovery, with China's high-income sectors continuing to expand steadily.
"There could be pent-up demand," said Jelena Sokolova from investment research firm Morningstar.
"People have a lot of savings, and once they feel better about their financial situation, they could go out and spend the money that has accumulated."
CHANGING MARKET
For traditional luxury brands, however, weakening consumer confidence is only part of the challenge.
The dominance of e-commerce means shoppers of all ages are accustomed to purchasing discounted products online within seconds from home.
Consumers now have a vast range of options, from platforms such as Taobao and JD.com to livestream shopping sessions on Xiaohongshu and Douyin, China's version of TikTok.
At Galeries Lafayette on Tuesday, as employees packed away stripped-down mannequins, Li said she believed the store had relied too heavily on "the traditional business model that has existed for decades in France".
"This new generation of Chinese likes to shop online," she said. "For a lot of people born in the 1990s and 2000s, this mall is a stranger."
Even when consumers visit physical stores, online prices continue to shape purchasing decisions.
"When I shop offline, after I spot an item of clothing, I'll see how much it costs to purchase online," said 46-year-old freelancer Yang Dunqin, who had visited the department store one last time.
Chinese consumers have "matured a lot", becoming more selective, said Jing Daily's Nan.
Younger shoppers, in particular, are placing less emphasis on established luxury labels and instead seeking out pop-up stores or emerging domestic brands.
"The need to buy luxury is no longer just a reflection of social status," she said. "It's really about self-expression."
Yang remained philosophical about the closure of the Galeries.
"It just shows that this era continues to move forward," he said. "It's part of changing times."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] 77 Immigration officers dismissed for disciplinary, integrity offences since 2022
Article:
KAJANG: A total of 77 Immigration Department officers have been dismissed over disciplinary and integrity-related offences since 2022, including eight officers sacked this year alone.
Immigration director-general Datuk Zakaria Shaaban said the department had resolved 971 disciplinary cases between 2022 and 2025, with dismissals among the actions taken against errant officers.
He said 23 disciplinary cases had been resolved so far this year.
"We will not compromise with any officer who violates integrity or commits misconduct.
"If we find them guilty based on evidence, disciplinary action will be taken, including dismissal," he said after the department's Wira Madani Semarak Aidiladha programme at the Millennium Immigration Depot in Beranang here today.
Zakaria said between 2022 and 2025, a total of 69 personnel were sacked for various disciplinary issues.
"This year, we have already sacked eight more," he said, adding that among the common offences were breaches of standard operating procedures (SOPs), abuse of authority, absenteeism, drug-related offences and smuggling prohibited items into immigration depots.
He said some officers were also found approving matters beyond their authority, including cases linked to "counter setting".
"Many people think action can be taken immediately, but disciplinary proceedings are subject to regulations and require concrete evidence.
"We cannot simply punish officers without proof because any decision must adhere to existing laws and regulations," he said.
He added that any decision made by the department's disciplinary board could also be challenged in court.
Zakaria said the 971 resolved disciplinary cases largely involved support group officers from Grade KP40 and below, as disciplinary matters involving management and professional officers fall under the Public Service Department's disciplinary board.
He added that other enforcement agencies, including the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission and police, had also taken separate action in cases involving criminal elements.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fed's Kashkari calls for focus on inflation risk, mum on timing of next rate move
Article:
TOKYO: The Federal Reserve must focus on containing inflationary risks that appear to be building, although it is "far too soon" to predict when it could next change interest rates, even as markets price in an October increase, Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said on Wednesday.
Kashkari also said the "inflationary shockwave" triggered globally by the Middle East war could persist, a concern that was already feeding into bond markets.
"We've had high inflation all around the world for five years now, with the conflict in Iran pushing up energy and related prices. It's affecting virtually every economy around the world," he said.
"I think that can explain a lot of the concern about inflation, which then obviously works its way into the bond market," he told Reuters in an interview in Tokyo.
When considering the fallout from the Middle East war on the US economy, the risk to inflation appeared greater than the risk of a weakening labour market, Kashkari said, although he added that the Fed must "pay attention to both".
While the US labour market remained in a "decent place", inflation was a real issue facing consumers daily, with rising energy prices expected to spill over into other parts of the economy with a lag, he said.
"The other really important factor is just that we've had elevated inflation for five years. That affects my willingness to look through what otherwise should be a temporary supply shock," Kashkari said.
Failing to respond to persistent price pressures risked fuelling public perceptions that the Fed was not serious about returning inflation to target, he added.
TOO SOON TO PREDICT RATE MOVE TIMING
Kashkari voted in favour of the Fed's decision to hold rates steady in April, but he was among those dissenting against maintaining dovish guidance that suggested the next move could be a rate cut.
The Fed should instead maintain neutral guidance indicating rates could either rise or fall depending on incoming data, he said.
"Since the dissent, which is now several weeks ago, I think most of the data has said the inflationary risks are higher, not lower."
Even if a quick deal were reached between the US and Iran, the impact on inflation would likely persist for some time because supply chains would take months to normalise, he said.
Inflationary pressures would also likely remain elevated globally as countries seek to replenish oil reserves, keeping upward pressure on prices.
Asked whether he still favoured guidance reflecting a neutral rather than hawkish stance, Kashkari said: "I think for now, yes. We need to see just what happens (with the Iran war). Negotiations are taking place and headlines are coming out every day. We have to see where those ultimately go."
With the energy shock adding to inflationary pressures, markets are pricing in the possibility of a Fed rate hike in October.
"I think it's far too soon for me to make such a prediction about when the next move would be," he said when asked about the October rate hike expectations.
"I want to see what happens in those negotiations and see how global supply chains are responding."
Asked about the Fed's decision-making under new chair Kevin Warsh, Kashkari said members of the Federal Open Market Committee would likely welcome hearing his views on the economy and policy.
"Everybody, I'm confident, will vote based on their own reading of the economy and what's appropriate, and ultimately it's going to be the best ideas that persuade the committee."
Concerns over large public debt burdens could also keep bond markets volatile, including in Japan and the US, he said.
Despite its large debt load, Japan may be in a safer position than some countries because most of its debt is held domestically, Kashkari said.
While US economic fundamentals remain strong, the country is on an "unsustainable" fiscal path over the long term that ultimately needs to be addressed by politicians, he added.
There are no clear signs yet of a debt-driven financial crisis, Kashkari said.
"But I also know there can be sudden moves in financial markets that can be destabilising."
Kashkari is visiting Tokyo to attend a conference on monetary policy hosted by the BOJ and its think tank.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Supply shock, debt may hurt central bank independence, ex-Fed executive says
Article:
TOKYO: Pressures on central bank independence are likely to increase as public dissatisfaction over supply shock-driven inflation may tempt politicians to assign blame to central banks, former Federal Reserve executive Donald Kohn said on Wednesday.
US President Donald Trump's efforts to pressure the Federal Reserve may be extreme, but they stem from broader political and economic developments across many democracies, he added.
Central banks face mounting challenges as supply shocks, such as the energy crisis caused by the Iran war, complicate efforts to maintain low and stable inflation, said Kohn, who remains in close contact with incumbent policymakers.
Falling real incomes would heighten public dissatisfaction and increase the temptation for political leaders to blame central banks, Kohn said in a speech to Japanese, US and European central bank executives attending a Bank of Japan-hosted conference in Tokyo.
"Ironically, these circumstances strengthen rather than weaken the case for independence," he said in a keynote lecture.
"When inflationary pressures arise from forces beyond monetary policy control, political incentives to demand short-term relief become stronger, even as the need for disciplined policy becomes greater."
An expected increase in already high public debt may also keep interest rates above growth rates for a period, Kohn said, pointing to Trump's calls for lower US interest rates as an attempt to influence the Fed "in part on budgetary grounds".
An extreme risk would be fiscal dominance, or a loss of confidence in the government's ability to manage debt, which in turn pressures the central bank to finance public borrowing.
"That is not our current situation, but fiscal pressure can threaten (central bank) independence well before outright fiscal dominance emerges," said Kohn, now a senior fellow at the Brookings Institution.
"Pressures on independence are likely to grow rather than recede," although central bank independence has endured because experience has repeatedly demonstrated its value, he said.
Central banks should protect themselves from attacks on their independence through "discipline, focus, institutional competence, humility about uncertainty, and a willingness to explain difficult choices clearly and candidly", he said.
Clear communication would also enhance credibility, he added, while warning central banks against relying too heavily on economic models.
"Models help organise a complicated world and illuminate historical relationships, but they are simplifications," he said.
"In practice, policy making requires something more, a narrative, a disciplined story about what is happening in the economy, what forces are driving outcomes, and how policy can respond."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Public concern over foreigners persists despite 17,313 illegal immigrant arrests, says Immigration DG
Article:
KAJANG: Public perception that foreigners are "taking over" parts of the country, especially in Kuala Lumpur, is often fuelled by large gatherings during weekends and festive holidays, despite many of them holding valid immigration documents.
Immigration director-general Datuk Zakaria Shaaban said hotspot areas such as KLCC and parts of the capital often see large groups of foreigners gathering during long weekends and festive seasons, leading some Malaysians to assume they are undocumented migrants.
"People see many foreigners in one place and perceptions arise that they are all illegal immigrants.
"But based on our inspections, many of them actually possess valid passes and permits," he said after the department's Wira Madani Semarak Aidiladha programme at the Millennium Immigration Depot in Beranang here today.
He said Malaysia currently has more than three million foreigners with valid immigration documents.
He added that some social media content creators could also be contributing to the perception by producing sensational content for higher engagement and publicity.
"We cannot deny that some content creators may be looking for sensational material. But society must understand that not every foreigner in this country is violating immigration laws," he said.
He said the department had conducted 5,010 enforcement operations nationwide this year as of May 25.
"The operations involved inspections of 71,734 individuals, resulting in the detention of 17,313 illegal immigrants for various immigration offences.
"Our department also arrested 789 employers suspected of harbouring or hiring foreigners without valid permits," he said, adding that most offences involved overstaying and misuse of immigration passes under the Immigration Act.
Statistics provided by the Immigration Department showed that the highest number of illegal immigrants detained were Indonesians (5,578), followed by Bangladeshis (3,533), Myanmar nationals (2,856), Filipinos (1,990) and Pakistanis (811).
Despite the large-scale operations, Zakaria said enforcement efforts would continue to be intensified as concerns over undocumented migrants remain among the public.
"The department will continue intensifying enforcement operations because there are still members of the public who are dissatisfied and feel there are too many foreigners in the country."
Zakaria added that undocumented migrants would usually flee once they spot Immigration enforcement vehicles.
The Wira Madani programme was held in conjunction with the Aidiladha celebrations and as an appreciation initiative for Immigration personnel serving as frontliners.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TNB solar initiative empowers mosques and local communities
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The use of solar energy not only symbolises advancements in green technology but reflects values that emphasise environmental stewardship, prudent resource management and community wellbeing.
Even in Islamic teachings, mankind is urged to protect the earth and avoid wastage. As such, the adoption of renewable energy such as solar is seen as aligned with efforts to build more sustainable and resilient communities.
Recognising this, Tenaga Nasional Bhd (TNB) and its subsidiary GSPARX Sdn Bhd have undertaken solar system initiatives to support more sustainable and environmentally friendly energy use at selected mosques nationwide through their corporate social responsibility (CSR) programme.
The use of solar energy is now opening a new chapter for mosques in the country, as the clean energy technology not only supports daily operations but strengthens the role of mosques as centres for local community development.
Among the mosques involved are Masjid Kampung Baru Pulau Redang, Masjid Al-Hijrah Tok Bali and Masjid An-Naim Berek 12, as well as Masjid Al-Huda Dungun, Masjid Kampung Dura Kuala Berang and Masjid Al-Amin Teluk Panglima Garang.
The installed solar system capacities range from 8.06 kilowatt peak (kWp) to 21.08 kWp, with some mosques also equipped with battery storage systems of up to 21 kilowatt-hours (kWh).
The initiative is expected to provide significant benefits to mosques through potential annual utility cost savings of between RM5,100 and RM14,677, depending on the installation capacity at each location.
In addition, the use of solar energy is expected to contribute to a reduction in carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions of between 6.39 tonnes and 17.25 tonnes annually, in line with the nation's sustainability and energy transition agenda.
Masjid Al-Amin Teluk Panglima Garang recorded the largest solar capacity installation at 21.08 kWp together with a 21 kWh battery system, with potential annual savings of RM14,677 and carbon avoidance of 17.25 tonnes per year.
Meanwhile, Masjid Kampung Dura Kuala Berang received a 17.36 kWp solar system with a 7 kWh battery, potentially generating annual savings of RM12,087.
Previously, TNB had also implemented a similar project at Masjid Saidina Umar Al Khattab on Sept 11, 2022.
Chairman of Masjid Saidina Umar Al-Khattab Kampung Tanjung Gahai  Yusiniamir Mat Amin said electricity bills had been significantly reduced since the installation of the solar system at the mosque
"The surplus funds are now being used for other activities such as religious classes and assisting congregants. We thank everyone involved, especially TNB.
"We hope this initiative will continue to benefit others," he said.
The mosque's treasurer Mohd Shariff Abdullah said monthly electricity bills were no longer a burden.
"Since the mosque adopted solar energy, it has greatly helped in our financial management.
"We are grateful for TNB's support and hope this effort will continue to benefit more communities," he said.
The ongoing effort reflects TNB's commitment to expanding the use of renewable energy at community premises and houses of worship, while supporting Malaysia's aspiration towards a low-carbon economy.
The programme is expected to serve as a catalyst for the adoption of green technology among local communities through the role of mosques as community institutions that remain close to the people.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muslims across country celebrate Aidiladha with prayers and takbir
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Muslims across the country today celebrated Hari Raya AidilAdha with gratitude and devotion as thousands of worshippers, including national and state leaders, filled mosques nationwide to perform their prayers.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim and his wife, Datuk Seri Dr Wan Azizah Wan Ismail, joined more than 10,000 congregants at Masjid Putra in Putrajaya. The sermon, titled "Pengorbanan Menuntut Keikhlasan", was delivered by Grand Imam Dr Muhammad Zakuwa Rodzali.
In
Selangor
, Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah joined more than 15,000 worshippers at Masjid Sultan Salahuddin Abdul Aziz Shah in Shah Alam.
He was accompanied by Tengku Permaisuri Norashikin, Raja Muda Tengku Amir Shah, Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari, and State Secretary Datuk Ahmad Fadzli Ahmad Tajuddin. A total of 28 cattle, including one contributed by the Sultan, were sacrificed.
[[nid:1449571]]
In
Kelantan
, Crown Prince Tengku Muhammad Fakhry Petra performed his prayers at Masjid Jamek Al-Ismaili in Bandar Baru Pasir Pekan, Tumpat, alongside Menteri Besar Datuk Mohd Nassuruddin Daud and the state leadership.
In
Kedah
, Sultan Al-Aminul Karim Sultan Sallehuddin Sultan Badlishah performed the prayers at Masjid Zahir in Alor Setar with more than 2,000 congregants, including Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Muhammad Sanusi Md Nor and State Secretary Datuk Seri Norizan Khazali.
In
Perlis
, Raja Muda Tuanku Syed Faizuddin Putra Jamalullail joined more than 1,200 congregants at Dataran Istiadat Istana Arau.
The sermon, titled "Pengorbanan Memajukan Umat Islam", was delivered by Perlis Islamic Religious and Malay Customs Council (MAIPs) chief executive officer Mohd Nazim Mohd Noor.
Also present were Raja Puan Muda Tuanku Dr Lailatul Shahreen Akashah Khalil and their daughters, Sharifah Khatreena Nuraniah and Sharifah Farah Adriana.
In
Negri Sembilan
, Yang Dipertuan Besar Tuanku Muhriz Tuanku Munawir and Tunku Ampuan Besar Tuanku Aishah Rohani Tengku Besar Mahmud arrived at Dewan Awam Tuanku Najihah in Kuala Pilah at 8.30am. Accompanied by Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun, they joined 1,500 congregants for the prayers.
In
Pahang
, Sultan Al-Sultan Abdullah Ri'ayatuddin Al-Mustafa Billah Shah and Tengku Ampuan Tunku Azizah Aminah Maimunah Iskandariah joined more than 1,000 worshippers at Masjid Tengku Ampuan Afzan in Indera Mahkota, accompanied by their children, including Regent Tengku Hassanal Ibrahim Alam Shah.
In
Perak
, Sultan Nazrin Shah performed his prayers at Masjid Sultan Idris Shah II in Ipoh with more than 1,000 congregants. Also in attendance were Raja Muda Raja Jaafar, Raja Di-Hilir Raja Iskandar Dzulkarnain, and Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Saarani Mohamad.
In
Johor
, Tunku Mahkota Ismail attended the prayers at Masjid Negeri Sultan Abu Bakar in Johor Baru alongside more than 1,000 congregants.
He was accompanied by his sons, Raja Muda Tunku Iskandar and Tunku Abu Bakar Ibrahim, as well as Tunku Panglima Tunku Abdul Rahman, Tunku Putera Tunku Abu Bakar, and Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi.
In
Terengganu
, Regent Tengku Muhammad Ismail Sultan Mizan Zainal Abidin performed his prayers at Masjid Al-Muktafi Billah Shah in Kuala Terengganu, accompanied by Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar.
In
Penang
, Governor Tun Ramli Ngah Talib and his wife, Toh Puan Raja Noora Ashikin Raja Abdullah, joined more than 5,000 Muslims at Masjid Abdullah Fahim in Bertam, Kepala Batas.
In
Melaka
, state Senior Executive Councillor Datuk Rais Yasin and Third Division Commander Major General Sani S. Omar joined more than 2,000 congregants, comprising military personnel and civilians, at Masjid As-Syuhada, Kem Terendak in Sungai Udang.
In
Sarawak
, Premier Tan Sri Abang Johari Tun Openg, alongside Deputy Premier Datuk Amar Awang Tengah Ali Hasan and State Legislative Assembly Speaker Tan Sri Mohamad Asfia Awang Nassar, joined more than 5,000 worshippers at Masjid Jamek Negeri Sarawak in Petra Jaya.
In
Sabah,
Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor performed his prayers with more than 1,000 congregants at Masjid Haji Noor in Kampung Serusup, Tuaran, before participating in sacrificial rituals where meat was distributed to local police personnel and asnaf groups.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New 500-bed Petaling Jaya Hospital to feature vertical design concept
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The proposed Petaling Jaya Hospital will be constructed as a vertical facility with a capacity of 500 beds.
Selangor Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the vertical design concept, previously implemented at the Ampang Hospital, would optimise land use and reduce costs for the Health Ministry.
He said the state government welcomed the announcement by Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad regarding the site selection for the public hospital.
"Efforts to secure a location for the Petaling Jaya public hospital have been actively pursued by the state government since 2018, taking into account the Petaling district's growing population, which has reached 2.3 million.
"In Petaling Jaya alone, the population exceeds 800,000, and residents currently depend on the Sungai Buloh Hospital, Shah Alam Hospital, and the University Malaya Medical Centre (UMMC)," Amirudin said in a statement today.
Dzulkefly was previously reported as saying that a six-hectare modern sports complex site, X Park PJ South in Taman Medan, had been identified as the location for the new hospital to serve residents in Petaling Jaya, Subang, and Puchong.
Amirudin said Sungai Buloh Hospital currently has a capacity of 1,037 beds, Hospital Shah Alam has 490 beds, and UMMC has 946 beds.
He added that the state government has consistently worked closely with the ministry to resolve overcrowding at public hospitals in Selangor, which led to the completion of Cyberjaya Hospital and Tanjong Karang Hospital in 2022.
"Cyberjaya Hospital has a capacity of 288 beds, while Tanjong Karang Hospital has 150 beds. Both were developed through land management collaboration between the state government and the ministry.
"Additionally, the 312-bed Kapar Hospital, scheduled for completion by the end of this year, is expected to alleviate congestion at Tengku Ampuan Rahimah Hospital in Klang and Shah Alam Hospital," he said.
Amirudin said the agreed site for Petaling Jaya Hospital is ideal for local communities in terms of accessibility, capacity, land costs, and its proximity to high-density residential areas.
A site visit by ministry representatives, state agencies, and local government officials, including the State Economic Planning Unit, the Petaling Jaya City Council, and the Petaling District Office, will take place this week.
He added that the state government remains committed to expediting all land-related processes to ensure the project is realised for the benefit of residents in Petaling Jaya, Subang Jaya, and Puchong.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tengku Zafrul: I'm ready to contest anywhere, but decision is PKR's to make
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Abdul Aziz is prepared to contest anywhere, including Pandan, in the upcoming elections following the resignation of Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli.
However, he said he would leave the decision up to PKR, while maintaining existing coordination with various groups, including PKR Youth and other grassroots organisations in the constituency.
"I am ready to contest anywhere if given the opportunity. (However), I will leave the decisions to the party leadership," he told reporters after the Korban Perdana Angkatan Muda Keadilan Pandan.
Earlier, PKR vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said Tengku Zafrul, who is the prime minister's senior political adviser, has been appointed as the party's monitoring officer for the constituency.
This comes after the constituency was
"adopted"
by Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim following Rafizi's resignation.
Also present at the event were Selangor Infrastructure and Agriculture Committee chairman Datuk Izham Hashim and Pandan PKR Youth chief Mohd Ifwat Amir Hamirudin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thai housing demand recovering but outlook clouded by energy shock risks
Article:
BANGKOK: Thailand's housing market showed recovery signs in the first quarter of 2026, the state house leader said on Wednesday, with transaction volumes rising on government stimulus even as gains in value lagged, underscoring weak purchasing power.
* The outlook remains fragile, with rising energy costs linked to Middle East war, soft domestic demand and a pullback in foreign buying expected to weigh on the sector through the rest of the year, the Government Housing Bank said in a statement.
* The housing market is expected to decline slightly in 2026 due to energy costs and inflation, though declines will be limited by government stimulus, an extended loan-to-value easing for another year and fee cuts for certain homes, the bank said.
* Foreign condominium demand weakened sharply in January-March, with transfers down about 17 per cent year-on-year in both volume and value, though foreigners still accounted for a significant share of transactions.
* Chinese buying declined steeply, by 43 per cent in value, while Russian demand grew, with foreign activity concentrated in Bangkok, Chonburi and Phuket, particularly in higher-end segments.
* The bank expects a mild contraction in 2026, with transfers seen down 1.1 per cent in volume and 2.3 per cent in value, and new mortgage lending projected to fall 1.6 per cent.
* Residential unit transfers rose 11.2 per cent year-on-year in the first quarter, while value increased a more modest 3.1 per cent, highlighting a shift towards lower-priced homes.
* Higher energy and construction costs, driven by geopolitical tensions, are weighing on household purchasing power, it said.
* Thailand's household debt was 16.44 trillion baht (US$504.45 billion) at the end of last year, or 86.7 per cent of GDP, among Asia's highest levels, dragging on consumption and growth.
* Housing loans are starting to recover after a prolonged downturn, with new mortgage lending rising 11.1 per cent year-on-year to about 122 billion baht in the first quarter.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Business as usual': Fakes, piracy thrive in Vietnam as US tariff deadline nears
Article:
HANOI: At a sprawling bazaar on the outskirts of Vietnam's capital, trade in counterfeit goods remains brisk despite government crackdowns and the threat of US tariffs linked to the issue, according to two visits by Reuters journalists to the market.
"Police come once a year with a TV crew. They film the seizure of a shop, and then it's business as usual," said a seller at her stall displaying fake Ralph Lauren polo shirts at Hanoi's Ninh Hiep wholesale market.
Ninh Hiep is among about 30 "notorious markets" worldwide identified by the US Trade Representative (USTR) in its latest annual report on counterfeiting and piracy.
The USTR also flagged streaming websites such as MyFlixerz, which it said are believed to operate from Vietnam and attract hundreds of millions of monthly visitors worldwide by offering pirated films and television series. Despite an announced crackdown, the sites remained accessible as of May 27.
The USTR and Vietnam's foreign ministry did not respond to requests for comment.
Washington regards intellectual property violations in Vietnam as serious and harmful to the US economy. On April 30, it designated the Southeast Asian nation as the world's worst offender on intellectual property rights and warned it could launch an investigation by the end of this month, potentially leading to trade tariffs.
The designation came as Vietnam's exports to the United States surged. In the first three months of this year, the US recorded a trade deficit with Vietnam of US$54.8 billion, larger than those with major exporters China and Mexico, according to US data. The Trump administration has repeatedly stated its intention to reduce trade deficits.
After the April designation as a "priority foreign country" for intellectual property rights, the only country added to the worst category in the US ranking in 13 years, Vietnam's foreign ministry said the country had made "strenuous efforts" to protect intellectual property and urged the United States to provide "an objective and balanced assessment of Vietnam's efforts and achievements".
### FAKES ON DEMAND
Shortly after the USTR announcement, the Vietnamese government launched a campaign against counterfeits and online piracy from May 7 to May 30.
It had launched a similar crackdown last year after the Trump administration unveiled tariffs of 46 per cent on imports from Vietnam, which have since been reduced to 10 per cent. Vietnam has been negotiating a trade agreement with Washington, its largest export market, over the past year.
Reuters reporters visited Ninh Hiep twice this month, once before the crackdown and again on May 25, and spoke with about 10 stallholders. All said authorities routinely carried out enforcement operations, but with limited long-term impact. They spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter.
One vendor said police had recently visited, prompting some shops to reduce displays of counterfeit branded goods, but added: "Fakes are still available in our storage if ordered."
Authorities responsible for anti-counterfeiting enforcement did not respond to requests for comment.
Reuters reporters observed dozens of stalls selling counterfeit clothing bearing brands including Ralph Lauren, Calvin Klein, Gucci, Gap and Alo Yoga. Many items carried tags in Chinese identifying manufacturers.
When asked, shop assistants acknowledged the products were counterfeit, with most imported from Guangzhou, China, and a smaller portion manufactured in Vietnam.
Past crackdowns have had limited impact, with some traders saying enforcement efforts and a new tax system had affected business.
A separate "notorious market" in Ho Chi Minh City was raided by police last year but remains operational. Motorcyclists continue to navigate Ninh Hiep's narrow alleys, purchasing goods for resale in central Hanoi and elsewhere.
"As long as there is demand, there will be supply," said one seller.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Shares scale new peaks as markets eye shaky US-Iran truce
Article:
TOKYO: Asian shares climbed to record highs while oil prices remained elevated on Wednesday, as markets looked for signals that a shaky truce between the United States and Iran will be extended.
The dollar held gains from the previous session after Iran said the US had violated a ceasefire. New Zealand's dollar rose after the central bank kept its policy rate unchanged but signalled that future hikes may need to come sooner.
Japanese and South Korean shares scaled fresh peaks after major US stock markets reopened from a holiday to rise to all-time highs on AI optimism. Sentiment remains vulnerable, however, as talks continue to reach a lasting halt to the three-month-long conflict that has rocked energy markets, and central bank comments will be watched for how the crisis is affecting the outlook for inflation and interest rates.
"The markets are just waiting for something tangible now when it comes to a deal between the US and Iran," Kyle Rodda, senior financial market analyst at Capital.com, wrote in a note.
"A lot of good news is priced in, leaving room for disappointment if something comprehensive isn't announced."
MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan advanced for a fifth straight session, rising 1.6 per cent to an all-time high. Japan's Nikkei rose 0.5 per cent, briefly trading above the 66,000 mark for the first time.
South Korea's KOSPI soared 3.41 per cent, extending gains after Samsung Electronics' unionised workers voted to approve a tentative wage deal, averting a strike that threatened to disrupt global chip supplies.
In early trade, pan-regional Euro Stoxx 50 futures were up 0.23 per cent, German DAX futures rose 0.24 per cent, while FTSE futures were down 0.18 per cent. US stock futures, the S&P 500 e-minis, edged up 0.06 per cent.
In currency markets, the dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies, was little changed at 99.07 after a 0.15 per cent gain in the prior session. The euro rose 0.12 per cent to US$1.1642, while the yen was steady at 159.25 per dollar.
Iran's foreign ministry said US strikes in the southern Hormozgan province represented a "gross violation" of a ceasefire. The US said its attacks were defensive in nature.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said a deal with Tehran to halt the conflict could "take a few days", while Iran's Tasnim news agency reported that Tehran was seeking the release of US$24 billion in funds frozen overseas.
US crude fell 1.97 per cent to US$92.04 a barrel, and Brent fell to US$98.07 per barrel, down 1.52 per cent after a nearly 4 per cent surge in the prior session.
The Reserve Bank of New Zealand held interest rates steady at 2.25 per cent in a split board decision that emphasised the need for rates to move up sooner. The kiwi jumped 0.7 per cent versus the greenback to US$0.5878.
In Australia, data showed consumer prices increased by less than expected in April while core inflation ticked up. The Australian dollar weakened 0.1 per cent to US$0.7161.
At a meeting of central bankers in Tokyo, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda cautioned that a temporary energy shock can become persistent if it feeds into wages and price-setting behaviour. European Central Bank board member Isabel Schnabel on Tuesday advocated an interest rate hike in June even if a US-Iran peace deal is reached.
The yield on benchmark US 10-year notes fell 1.8 basis points to 4.473 per cent, down for a third day to the lowest since May 14. On a light day for economic data, markets were looking forward to Thursday's release of the personal consumption expenditures (PCE) index, the measure favoured by the Federal Reserve for setting its 2 per cent annual inflation target.
"With the PCE print due and geopolitical tensions still simmering around the Strait of Hormuz, this week could be a pivotal one for rate expectations, the dollar, oil, and gold alike," said Lukman Otunuga, head of market research at FXTM.
Spot gold rose 0.1 per cent to US$4,510.82 an ounce, while copper rose 0.55 per cent to US$13,699.00 a metric ton.
In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin fell 0.40 per cent to US$75,711.92, and ether declined 0.26 per cent to US$2,070.46.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pro-Net expands local EV digital ecosystem efforts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: PRO-Net, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Proton Holdings Bhd, is expanding localisation efforts for its electric vehicle (EV) ecosystem through its Proton e.Mas brand, focusing on digital infrastructure, connected mobility services and charging integration.
The company said the initiative aligns with national policies including the National Automotive Policy and the National Energy Transition Roadmap, while supporting broader efforts to develop Malaysia's EV ecosystem.
Chief executive officer Zhang Qiang said Proton e.Mas is focusing on services designed around local driving habits, infrastructure and user needs.
According to Pro-Net, Proton e.Mas is part of a connected vehicle platform serving over 300,000 vehicles, including Proton models.
The platform supports features such as remote vehicle control, connected infotainment, charging integration and mobile app services.
The company said more than eight million remote-control requests for EV-related services have been recorded to date.
The connected ecosystem includes Bahasa Malaysia language support, localised navigation and a live charging map.
Other features include a Qibla compass and integration with the TheNoor prayer application.
Pro-Net also said its infotainment cockpit hardware is fully manufactured locally.
The company has partnered with local technology and telecommunications firms including Telekom Malaysia Bhd (TM), Maxis Bhd and CelcomDigi Bhd as well as digital service providers and charge point operators.
Pro-Netsaid it has invested more than RM40 million into cloud services and its connectivity platform, which is hosted at TM data centres in Malaysia to support local data storage requirements.
Separately, the company said it has invested over RM4 million into its charging platform.
Proton e.Mas users currently have access to more than 4,300 charging points nationwide through its integrated charging map, covering about 90 per cent of Malaysia's public charging network.
The charging platform has recorded around 11,000 users, 30,000 charging orders and about 790,000 kWh in charging volume to date.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-cop finds true meaning of sacrifice after losing leg in crash
Article:
TASEK GELUGOR: Every Hari Raya Aidiladha, Muslims reflect on the meaning of sacrifice, resilience and submission to Allah's will.
For former Penang special branch officer Assistant Superintendent Abd Samad Kasan, the lesson became painfully real after a horrific accident last year claimed his left leg — yet not his spirit to carry on.
The 68-year-old retiree described the tragedy as the greatest test of his life, one that transformed his understanding of "korban" beyond the ritual of Aidiladha.
Instead of surrendering to despair, Samad chose to rise.
Just 26 days after his leg was amputated following a collision with a lorry in Kampung Selamat here on Aug 19, he began rebuilding his life with quiet determination.
"Life must go on. If we keep looking back at what we have lost, we will never find the strength to move forward.
"Alhamdulillah, Allah still gave me a chance to live. That itself is a blessing," he said when met at his home in Kampung Nyior Sebatang here.
The Batu Pahat-born former policeman said his 35 years in the force had taught him discipline, endurance and mental strength — qualities that became crucial after the accident.
He joined the police force in 1981 before serving in the Special Branch from 1996. He retired with the rank of ASP in 2016.
Although he never allowed himself to succumb to hopelessness, he admitted the emotional scars lingered in the months after the incident.
"For the first three months, I avoided the road where the accident happened. Every time I saw the place, the memory would return instantly.
"But slowly, I forced myself to face it. Today, I can pass through the area again without fear," he added.
Now living with his wife, 12-year-old daughter and mother-in-law, Samad continues to lead an independent life, including driving on his own despite his disability.
He said the support from his family and former colleagues became a source of strength during his darkest moments.
"What touched me most was knowing that even after retirement, my former colleagues and friends still remembered me and came to visit.
"That support gave me the strength to keep going," he said.
Abd Samad was met after receiving a visit from Penang Special Branch chief Senior Assistant Commissioner Zakaria A. Rahim, representing the Penang police chief, alongside Yayasan Kalsom Bivi chairman Datuk Bahurdin Abdul Kader.
They presented cash aid and a wheelchair to assist him in his daily life.
Zakaria said the sacrifices and contributions of retired police personnel would never be forgotten.
"They served the country with dedication and courage. Their welfare will always remain close to our hearts," he said.
Meanwhile, Bahurdin said he was deeply saddened when informed of Samad's condition by the retiree's wife, Suraini Bidin, 54, as they had known each other for more than two decades.
"Despite losing a leg, he never lost his fighting spirit. That is what makes his story truly inspiring," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hong Kong watchdog raids local units of two Chinese brokerages
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Kong securities regulator has raided the local arms of two major Chinese brokerages as it investigates suspected misconduct tied to share offerings, sources said, the latest move by  authorities to ramp up policing of an initial public offering (IPO) boom in the city.
The Securities and Futures ​Commission (SFC) searched the Hong Kong offices of CCB International (CCBI) and China Securities International (CSCI) as part of its probe, said the sources with knowledge of the matter.
CCB International and China Securities International are the offshore units of Beijing-headquartered China Construction Bank Corp  and China Securities Co , respectively.
The sources declined to be named as they were not authorised to speak to the media.
Spokespersons for the SFC and CSCI declined to comment, CCBI did not respond to Reuters request for comment. It's not immediately known which share offerings were the focus of the latest regulatory probe.
The probe highlights a broader regulatory crackdown in Hong Kong, where a surge in IPO activity - propelling the city to the top of global fundraising rankings last year - has strained banks and exposed gaps in due diligence and listing practices.
In March, the SFC launched one of the biggest crackdown on the investment banking sector in the last decade, raiding at least two Chinese securities brokerage firms, a hedge fund, and arresting eight individuals.
That came a couple of months after the watchdog warned it had identified "serious deficiencies" in stock market listing applications, prompting it to halt some applications, tighten scrutiny and order banks to review their procedures.
In the first quarter, Hong Kong, which is the most preferred venue for Chinese companies looking to raise capital offshore, saw companies raising HK$109.9 billion (US$14.03 billion) via IPOs, making it the world's No.1 new share sale venue.
As part of the latest investigation, Hong Kong SFC officials raided the local offices of the two Chinese brokerages and took away some documents and electronic devices, said the sources.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unduk Ngadau finalist puts spotlight on Dusun Labuk language
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Spectators may get the rare opportunity to hear the Dusun Labuk language at the state-level Kaamatan Unduk Ngadau pageant this Sunday.
If finalist Cyra Eva Bella Stephen makes it into the top 25, she will converse in her mother tongue during the question-and-answer session at the Hongkod Koisaan in Penampang.
Following the registration of the 53 finalists on Tuesday, the 21-year-old will also use her native language for a recorded self-introduction session, and when interacting with fellow contestants and organisers until May 31.
The finalist from Kampung Gambaron, Telupid has put in extensive effort to ensure linguistic accuracy and authenticity, noting that the language involves challenging pronunciation despite her using it since youth.
Dusun Labuk is primarily spoken in Telupid and Beluran along the Labuk River. It serves as a vital ethnic identity for local communities comprising subgroups such as the Sogilitan, Turavid, Tompulung, Tindakon, and Putih.
Cyra relies on her mother, Roth Jukin Kurum, to guide her through unfamiliar or confusing words.
She noted that her tendency to mix dialects stems from her father being a Dusun from Tamparuli.
"For example, the Dusun Labuk word for language is 'tangar', while in the Dusun Liwan dialect it is 'boros'.
"Through this platform, I want more people to hear and recognise Dusun Labuk," said Cyra, who wore a traditional Dusun Labuk "Sabung Impit Livogu" attire from the Turavid subgroup when met here.
The Unduk Ngadau committee has made mother-tongue proficiency a requirement for over two decades, while the emphasis on authentic traditional attire was introduced in 2015.
Cyra is among the 53 finalists vying to be crowned "Huminodun" this year.
While recent population data remains limited, a 1980s study by Canadian linguist Hope Hurlbut estimated that between 12,000 and 14,000 people spoke Dusun Labuk.
Aware of the risk of language extinction, Cyra continues to set an example by actively using her mother tongue within her community.
In 2024, Summer Institute of Linguistics (SIL) Malaysia executive director Dr Timothy Philips warned that many indigenous languages in Sabah risk disappearing due to declining intergenerational use.
He noted that a community loses its
core identity
once its language is gone.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hengyuan Refining returns to profit with RM525.55mil Q1 gain
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hengyuan Refining Company Bhd returned to the black in the first quarter ended March 31, 2026 (Q1 FY26), posting a net profit of RM525.55 million from a net loss of RM170.44 million a year ago.
In a filing to Bursa Malaysia, the group said the stronger performance was underpinned by higher production and sales volumes, supported by stable plant operations and disciplined execution, as well as lower finance costs.
Revenue more than doubled to RM4.62 billion in Q1 from RM2.40 billion previously, driven by higher average selling prices across major products and increased sales volumes.
Earnings per share improved to 83.39 sen, compared with a loss per share of 51.61 sen in the corresponding quarter last year.
Looking ahead, Hengyuan Refining said the downstream refining outlook for the financial year ending Dec 31, 2026, will continue to be shaped by global supply and demand dynamics.
It added that refining margins are expected to remain sensitive to changes in supply conditions, seasonal demand trends, and developments in key refining facilities and producing regions.
"While market uncertainties and global oil price volatility are expected to persist, the company will continue to monitor conditions closely, strengthen financial risk management and hedging strategies, and focus on operational efficiency and plant stability to capture opportunities and improve overall performance," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two men stabbed at LG Electronics office in Seoul; suspect in custody
Article:
SEOUL: Two men were stabbed Wednesday at an LG Electronics office in western Seoul, with the suspect currently in custody, authorities said, Yonhap News Agency reported.
The men – one in his 50s and the other in his 40s – were found stabbed on the second floor of the company's Magok business centre in Gangseo Ward after a report was filed at 11.18am, according to police and firefighting officials.
Both have been taken to hospitals with injuries to their arms and ribs, but neither was in life-threatening condition, they said.
The suspect fled the scene and was later apprehended near Digital Media City Station, police said. He claimed to have been heading to a police station to turn himself in.
The suspect is said to be an employee of a partner company, and reportedly claimed to have been harassed by the victims before attacking them out of sudden anger.
Police said they plan to question the suspect to determine the details. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former President Biden sues DOJ over release of interview audio
Article:
UNITED STATES: Former Democratic US President Joe Biden sued the US Department of Justice on Tuesday, seeking to bar the release of audio recordings and transcripts of private conversations with his biographer in 2016 and 2017.
The lawsuit, filed in federal court in Washington DC, comes ahead of the department's planned June 15 release of the materials to the US House Judiciary Committee and the conservative Heritage Foundation.
The foundation sought them after they were used as part of then-Special Counsel Robert Hur's 2023 investigation into Biden's handling of classified documents. Hur declined to bring criminal charges.
The department fought the Heritage Foundation's 2024 request for the records as exempt from the Freedom of Information Act until President Donald Trump took office, the lawsuit claims.
It announced it would be releasing the records in response to the committee's request, which the lawsuit claims is meant only to skirt federal law barring their release.
The lawsuit asks the court to declare the committee's request pretextual and invalid, and permanently bar the release of the records to the committee.
Representatives for the Department of Justice did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The recordings, made in Biden's home, were part of the writing process for his 2017 memoir, "Promise Me, Dad: A Year of Hope, Hardship, and Purpose", which detailed Biden's decision to pursue the presidency while his eldest son Beau fought brain cancer.
Earlier this month, Biden sought to intervene in the Heritage Foundation's lawsuit against the Justice Department over the materials. Last week, a judge allowed Biden to join the case but barred him from pursuing claims about the committee's request for the materials, according to court records.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: The PR accreditation debate: Are we solving the right problem?
Article:
LETTERS:
After more than six decades of advocating accreditation for Malaysia's PR industry, perhaps the more important question is not whether accreditation is needed, but why the idea has never truly gained broad industry traction.
If the proposal had achieved wider operational consensus, the industry would likely have rallied behind it more organically over time.
The reality may simply be that Malaysia's communications landscape is far too broad, fragmented and operationally diverse to be meaningfully shaped through the perspective of any single organisation alone.
Today's ecosystem extends far beyond traditional PR, spanning agencies, in-house teams, crisis managers, government communicators, ESG advisers, digital strategists, content creators, academics and media professionals – all operating within vastly different realities and pressures.
Yet discussions surrounding accreditation have often appeared to require broader practitioner participation, particularly from practitioners managing real-world reputational, regulatory and stakeholder challenges daily.
Accreditation is not merely symbolic; it carries implications for legitimacy, standards, influence and potential regulation, far too significant to be shaped through selective conversations alone.
The call to professionalise the communications industry is valid and timely amid growing concerns over misinformation, AI-driven manipulation, influencer persuasion and declining public trust.
The current debate should therefore not be viewed as opposition to professionalisation itself, but as an opportunity to broaden the conversation around what the industry now requires in a rapidly evolving communications environment.
The industry certainly needs stronger ethics, accountability and competency standards. But professionalism and compulsory accreditation are not automatically the same thing.
